5. Representation Theory Review

Big picture:

  • Groups divide vector spaces into non-interacting subspaces.
  • These non-interacting subspaces are irreducible representatioxns (irreps).
  • Any vector space a group acts on can be expressed in terms of these irreps.
  • We find irreps using …

Linear layers have to obey Schur’s lemma. Theorems show us how to enumerate all possible representations.

Read 702 notes.

5.1. Notation

  • Let denote a representation indexed by .

5.2. Great Orthogonality Theorem

Theorem 3 (Great Orthogonality Theorem). For a finite group , fix a complete set of pairwise non-isomorphic irreducible unitary representations (one representative per isomorphism class), and for each fix an orthonormal basis of its space (so is a fixed unitary matrix for each ). Then for all and indices For all pairs of irreps and , we have the following orthonormality relation:

(21)

Dresselhaus notation and proof

Go through it step by step. Case 2 uses the cyclic trace identity.

Einsum implementation

Does not check unitarity

def check_orthogonality_theorem(irreps: List[np.array]):
    """Checks orthogonality theorem for a set of input representations.
    Input:
        irreps: List of representations, np.arrays of shape [n, d, d], where n is the order of group and d is the dimension of the representation. Not necessarily irreducible!
    Output:
        True if the theorem holds (i.e. the representations in the list are irreducible, unitary and pairwise orthogonal and have the appropriate self-inner product), False otherwise.
    """
    for (irrep1, irrep2) in itertools.combinations(irreps, 2):
        if (not np.allclose(np.einsum('gab,gcd->acbd', np.conj(irrep1), irrep2), 0.0)):
            return False
    for irrep in irreps:
        order = irrep.shape[0]
        d = irrep.shape[1]
        if (not np.allclose(np.einsum('gab,gcd->acbd', np.conj(irrep), irrep), ((order / d) * np.einsum('ac,bd->acbd', np.eye(d), np.eye(d))))):
            return False
    return True

Remarks

  • Geometric interpretation: The entries of the irreps live in a -dimensional vector space and are orthogonal under the standard inner product. For a rep of dimension , there are choices for . Hence linear independence implies

    (22)

    It turns out by the Main Theorem that we get equality.

  • The character orthogonality relation in the Main theorem can be derived from the Great Orthogonality Theorem. Just write out the trace summtations.

    Proof

    From “Great orthogonality theorem …” on Notablity.

Corollary 1. A necessary and sufficient conditions for two irreps to be equivalent is that their characters are the same. (Holds immediately from orthogonality of irreps)

5.3. Irrep Properties

Here we present some more facts that are related to the Main theorem. Basically the characters of isomorphism classes of irreducible representations form an orthonormal basis of class functions.

  • Since any representation can be block diagonlized into irreps, its character is a linear combination of irrep characters. For a given representation, how do we determine the multiplicity of each irrep? Just use the orthogonality relation:

    Dresselhaus formula
  • The number of irreps equals the number of conjugacy classes. This is consistent with the fact that irreps form a basis for conjugacy class functions.

5.4. [REVIEW] Algorithm for finding irreps

Goal: How to find the irreps themselves if we don’t already have the character table, and how to find the change-of-basis matrix that block-diagonalizes the representation.

Strategy: (Let )

  • Find a “generic enough”2 matrix that commutes with the representation, then diagonalize .
  • By Schur’s lemma, the eigenspaces of are the irreducible subspaces we’re looking for (think about it).
  • Then each irrep is the restriction of onto that subspace. We can compute this by getting an orthonormal basis for each subspace . Then . is the irrep and is the change of basis matrix.
Algorithm Code
def decompose_rep_into_irreps(rep: np.array, *, tol: float=1e-08) -> List[np.array]:
    """Decomposes representation into irreducible representations.
    Input:
        rep: np.array [n, d, d] representation of group. rep[g] is a matrix that
            represents g-th element of group.
    Output:
        Irreducible representations. List of np.array [n, d_i, d_i] where d_i is a dimension of i-th irrep.
                                     Note: you can output the irreps in any order and in any basis.
                                     If an irrep is included multiple times in the decomposition (i.e. with multiplicity greater than 1), please simply include it multiple times in the output list.
    """
    Q_basis = linalg.infer_change_of_basis(rep, rep)
    m = len(Q_basis)
    print(Q_basis.shape)
    w = np.random.rand(m)
    Q_bar = np.einsum('m,mab->ab', w, Q_basis)
    print(Q_bar.shape)
    eigenspaces = linalg.eigenspaces(*np.linalg.eig(Q_bar))
    
    irreps = []

    for λ, B in eigenspaces:
        B = linalg.gram_schmidt(B.T)[0].T
        Bdag = np.conj(B.T)
        irreps.append(np.einsum('ab, nbc, cd -> nad', Bdag, rep, B))
    return irreps

Example 1 (Irreps of ). The characters are the functions

(23)

by the character orthogonality relation

5.5. [REVIEW] Tensor Product and Clebsch-Gordan

We want a canonical way to build higher-order features and nonlinear interactions while preserving equivariance. There are two standard constructions of multiplying representations.

  1. Tensor product of two representations of the same group :

    Given two representations of the same group and

    (24)

    is a representation of on .

  2. Direct product of representations of two commuting groups and :

    Given two representations and

    (25)

    is a representation of on .

    [TODO] read https://piazza.com/class/mkvdcqtb4w56id/post/33, I think the commuting is wrong here.

We discuss (1) for the rest of this section and (2) in the next section.

Even if and are both irreps of , the tensor product is in general reducible. Finding these coefficients and the corresponding change-of-basis matrix is the Clebsch–Gordan problem3. We can bash it by using the algorithm from Section 5.4.

Clebsch–Gordan tells you the correct, symmetry-compatible ways to combine two vectors:

Example 2. You have a network that sees a molecule. If you rotate the molecule, you want the predicted dipole moment to rotate the same way (equivariance), and you want a predicted energy to stay the same (invariance).

Suppose at some layer you have two vector features attached to an atom. Under a rotation , they transform as , Now you want to “multiply” them to create richer features.

If you do a naive coordinatewise product, this is not equivariant in general. Taking the tensor product and then projecting onto irreps is equivariant by Proposition 5

Proposition 5 (Tensor product followed by decomposition is equivariant). The map

(26)

is equivariant.

Proof

The first arrow is equivariant by definition Decomposing is an intertwiner by construction. Composing two equivariant maps is equivariant.

□

Remark. This tensor product followed by decomposition is what we will commonly refer to as the “tensor product” operation in equivariant neural networks. It describes how features in two different representations interact to produce features in other representations.

5.6. (Internal) Direct product

Direct product is for modelling different symmetries that commute (intuition: ).

Let and be two groups such that all operations of commute with all operations of (This can makes sense if they are subgroups of a bigger group) and . The direct product of these two groups is all elements in the form . Convince yourself that this has the right cardinality and is a group4.

Theorem 4. The direct product of representations of two groups and satisfyign above forms a representation of the direct product group. That is,

Example 3.

If you design to satisfy rotation-equivariance and translation-equivariance, then it is equivariant over the whole group.
